title: Birth confound disambiguation for VERY_NARROW [0.7,1.3] clip bounds

content.hypothesis: Entry 042 found VERY_NARROW [0.7,1.3] has d=7.583 improvement over canonical [0.3,1.8]. This could be a birth confound: tighter bounds force alpha initialization into a narrower range near 1.0, independent of ongoing adaptation.
content.method: Four-condition factorial design with matched seeds (n=5, K=[4,6,8,10], n_perm=8, n_trials=6): A) Canonical init+clip=[0.3,1.8] (baseline), B) VN-Full init+clip=[0.7,1.3] (full effect), C) VN-InitOnly init=[0.7,1.3] clip=[0.3,1.8] (birth confound isolation), D) VN-ClipOnly init=[0.3,1.8] clip=[0.7,1.3] (genuine binding isolation). Init bounds control where alpha starts; clip bounds control ongoing constraint.
content.result: PRIMARILY GENUINE BINDING. Both birth confound and genuine binding are significant, but ongoing clip constraint dominates. d_clip=+7.234 >> d_init=+2.794 (ratio 2.59x, above 1.5x threshold). Entry 042 fully replicated (d=+7.583, p=0.000). The ongoing clip constraint accounts for ~97% of the MI gap improvement; birth distribution accounts for ~30% but is largely washed out. The clip constraint itself is the binding structural variable, not initialization.

metrics.clip_effect_pct: 96.7% of full effect from clip constraint
metrics.init_effect_pct: 30.1% of full effect from birth distribution

content.root_cause: The VN clip constraint [0.7,1.3] forces alpha to stay near 1.0 throughout adaptation (not just at birth). Alpha values near 1.0 produce higher MI gap because the nonlinearity operates in a more sensitive regime. The birth confound adds a secondary contribution (~30%) but the ongoing constraint dominates.
